---
output: 
  pdf_document:
    citation_package: natbib
    keep_tex: false
    fig_caption: true
    toc: false
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    template: /Users/serpent/Documents/R Resources/Markdown Temps/svm-latex-ms.tex
bibliography: master.bib
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{pdfpages}
  - \AtBeginDocument{\includepdf[pages=1 scale=1, fitpaper=true]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Dissertation/images/cover/cover.pdf}}
  - \usepackage{array}   
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{siunitx}
  - \usepackage[table]{xcolor}
  - \definecolor{navyblue}{RGB}{80, 80, 80} 
  - \definecolor{darkblue}{RGB}{0, 0, 139}  
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{hhline}
  - \usepackage{calc}
  - \usepackage{tabularx}
  - \usepackage{fontawesome}
  - \usepackage[para,online,flushleft]{threeparttable}
  - \usepackage{setspace}
  - \setstretch{1.5}
  - \usepackage{helvet}
  - \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
  - \usepackage{fancyhdr}
  - \usepackage{etoolbox}
  - \setlength{\headheight}{38.68912pt}  
  - \addtolength{\topmargin}{-20pt}  
  - \setlength{\headsep}{20pt} 
  - \AtBeginEnvironment{thebibliography}{\linespread{1.5}\selectfont}
  - \DeclareCaptionFont{navyblue}{\color{navyblue}}  
  - \captionsetup{font={stretch=1.25,small,navyblue,sf}, labelfont={bf,navyblue}, textfont=sf, justification=justified, singlelinecheck=false}
  - \sloppy                  
  - \emergencystretch=3em      
  - \hyphenpenalty=500        
  - \tolerance=1000           
  - \exhyphenpenalty=1000  
  - \usepackage{float}  
  - \pagestyle{fancy}  
  - \fancyhf{} 
  - \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}  
  - \fancyhead[L]{}  
  - \fancyhead[R]{} 
  - \fancyhead[C]{\includegraphics[width=1cm]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Dissertation/images/portico.png}} 
  - \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}  
  - \usepackage{hyperref} 
  - \usepackage[toc,page]{appendix}
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \titleformat{\section}[block]{\normalsize\bfseries}{SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL}{1em}{}
  - \renewcommand{\bibsection}{\section*{\MakeUppercase{REFERENCES}}}
biblio-style: apalike
title: "Space Over Time: Functional Traits Reveal Environmental Feedbacks Driving Forest Succession"
abstract: " **Abstract:** Succession is a fundamental driver of tree diversity and closely links to forest productivity, vulnerability, and resilience. Environmental changes are altering successional dynamics, raising the need to better understand succession across large spatial and temporal scales. However, a key challenge lies in unravelling the importance, mechanisms, and predictability of feedback dynamics between trees and their environment. In this study, we partitioned succession into trajectories of eight traits representing key functional clusters in trees, providing the first comprehensive view of trait succession across environments. We used machine learning to model trait trajectories across forest stand age, while accounting for the effects of abiotic conditions. We then compared trait trajectories under scenarios of high and low conditions to infer the importance, mechanisms, and predictability of abiotic conditions driving succession. Our results show that temperature and elevation are the dominant predictors of most traits, limiting functional turnover except in traits primarily associated with resource competition. Climate conditions and elevation strongly and deterministically filter functional groups at the outset of succession, but also modulate successional trajectories, driving community divergence across different environments. Temperature-induced divergence may therefore be a previously unrecognized consequence of climate change, underscoring the need to establish relationships between global warming and community divergence to better prepare for successional shifts and to develop adaptive management strategies."
keywords: "Trait Succession, Community Assembly, Machine Learning, Random Forest"
geometry: margin=1in
fontsize: 12pt
endnote: no
linkcolor: darkblue
citecolor: darkblue
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, echo = FALSE)
```

\newpage

# INTRODUCTION

Forests are hotspots of biodiversity and provide vital ecosystem services like carbon storage, regulation of water and nutrient cycles, or prevention of soil degradation [@mori2017]. After a century of significant forest loss [@curtis2018; @kumar2022], deforestation rates have decelerated since the 1990s, while planted forests have increased by over 120 million hectares [@faoGlobalForestResources2020a]. Nevertheless, over 85% of global forests remain threatened according to the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems criteria [@ferrer-paris2019]. One reason is the cumulative effect of land use and, more recently, climate change [@curtis2018] in shifting successional dynamics [@andrewsa2018]. This poses a particular threat to regenerating secondary forests, the dominant forest type globally [@faoGlobalForestResources2020a], which have widely scattered age distributions and developmental stages [@besnard2021]. Therefore, understanding successional pathways is a cornerstone in contemporary ecology [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023] and has been a foundational objective for decades [@chang2019; @christensen2014; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. The relationships between succession and the environment are especially pertinent to forestry and forest conservation, as forest productivity, sensitivity to threats, and the scale of ecosystem services they provide vary across succession [@andrewsa2018; @haeussler2021; @malhiAllocationEcosystemNet2011; @sanchez-pinillos2019]. Therefore, successional pathways guide conservation prioritization [@christensen2014; @odum1969] and inform how ecosystem services can be enhanced, vulnerabilities mitigated, and the resilience of forests promoted in the face of increasing land use pressures and climate change [@christensen2014; @mori2017; @odum1969].\

\noindent In secondary forests, succession describes how communities assemble and change over time following a discrete disturbance event [@chang2019; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. Most successional theories recognize a sequential turnover in tree species composition and functional groups over time, often separating *early*, *intermediate*, and *late* stages of succession [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. Transitions between stages are commonly understood in the context of community assembly and species co-existence theories [@chang2016; @hillerislambers2012], which suggest that transitions are driven by feedback dynamics between trees and their biotic or abiotic environments [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. For instance, low competition and favourable light conditions during early succession generally promote high species diversity and functional traits oriented towards resource acquisition [@skutch1929; @swanson2011]. Increased competition during intermediate succession often leads to stem exclusion, i.e., the establishment of taller trees and canopy closure [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. This limits resource availability in late-stage succession, ultimately stabilizing a complex community structure characterized by traits that emphasize resource conservation, robustness, and longevity [@lebrija-trejos2010a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @sanchez-pinillos2019].\

\noindent Since feedback mechanisms should create predictable trajectories, successional pathways have long been considered orderly, directional, and determined by mostly autogenic factors [@christensen2014; @odum1969]. Ergo, successional pathways were typically inferred from chronosequences, i.e., space-for-time substitutions [@johnson2008a]. However, research of the past 50 years observed a substantial variability in succession [@christensen2014] across environments and within similar stands [@chazdon2006; @feldpausch2007a; @johnson2008a; @vandermeer2004], challenging the robustness of chronosequences for drawing successional inferences [@chang2016; @christensen2014; @norden2015]. Looking forward, the creation of novel assemblages by climate change [@ordonez2016] is likely to introduce further variability in successional patterns when species adapt to changing conditions [@vantiel2024].\

\noindent Identifying unifying trends in succession across temporal and spatial scales is therefore crucial for holistically understanding successional pathways and continuously forecasting them under novel conditions [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. However, traditional approaches to quantify trends through taxonomy face several limitations. First, feedback dynamics are most often related to traits or phylogeny [@levinsEvolutionChangingEnvironments2020; @kraft2010]. Second, due to the long timescales involved in succession, most taxonomic studies rely on correlative approaches that often depend on simulations or null models to infer underlying mechanisms. These approaches are contentious, as they can easily conflate different ecological processes, leading to potential misinterpretations [@connor1979]. Third, taxonomic approaches struggle in novel environments where no-analog communities may emerge [@williamsProjectedDistributionsNovel2007]. Functional traits offer a way forward because they provide a mechanistic perspective on species turnover and stage transitions while escaping taxonomic constraints [@kraft2015; @kraft2015a]. By partitioning succession into functional trajectories, it becomes possible to assess the importance, mechanisms, and determinism of feedback dynamics in community assembly along distinct axes in trait space [@douma2012].\

\noindent The relative importance of environment-tree feedbacks, i.e., abiotic conditions governing tree assembly [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024; @kraft2015a], may for example be inferred from a trait's signal throughout succession [@grime1988; @ackerly2007]. If abiotic conditions strongly selects for trait expressions that ensure success in a given environment, they inhibit functional turnover over time [@kraft2015a]. Selected traits are then static contributors to a tree's survival chance throughout succession, reflecting adaptation to environmental constraints, with the successional stage of the forest having little impact on survival. Conversely, a high successional signal indicates a high functional turnover [@kraft2010], and trait trajectories to be predominantly governed by changing interactions between co-occurring species [@chesson2000; @kunstler2016; @mayfield2010; @kraft2010]. These traits are dynamic contributors to a tree's survival chances, and certain expressions can be more or less favourable at different successional stages. \

\noindent Moreover, the underlying mechanisms of environment-tree feedbacks may be inferred from differences between trait trajectories across environments [@boukili2017; @kraft2015a; @lebrija-trejos2010]. For example, abiotic conditions could either act as environmental filters, selecting for trait expressions at the outset of succession and thereby determine which trees are able to establish and persist initially [@kraft2015a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. They could also modulate successional pathways of traits across time if trajectories differ with abiotic conditions, leading to divergence or convergence in community assembly between environments as succession progresses [@kraft2015a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @douma2012]. Both mechanisms may operate independently or synergistically, with the abiotic conditions both filtering the outset and continuously modulating successional trajectories.\

\noindent Additionally, the determinism of environment-tree feedbacks may be inferred from their predictability, offering an additional axis to identify traits driving successional variability across environments [@arroyo-rodriguezLandscapescaleForestCover2023; @norden2015]. A trait's predictability may generally be high but can vary across environments with different conditions due to either filtering, where similar trajectories have different starting points, or modulation, where trajectories diverge over time [@douma2012; @kraft2015a]. Less predictable traits may still respond to abiotic conditions but follow more stochastic paths, suggesting a greater role for allogenic factors or neutral processes [@hubbellUnifiedNeutralTheory2011; @boukili2017]. Integrating the predictability of trait feedbacks with their underlying mechanisms offers insights into how novel communities may emerge under environmental change and the extent to which these communities may assemble deterministically. [@guoClimateWarmingLeads2018].\

\noindent Understanding the relative importance, mechanisms, and predictability of environmental feedback dynamics driving trait trajectories could be a step towards a unified theory of succession [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024], ultimately enhancing management strategies for forests facing the impacts of climate change [@andrewsa2018; @vantiel2024]. However, a lack of sufficiently large, homogeneous datasets across traits, space, and time has so far limited our understanding of trait succession and the confounding effects of environmental feedback dynamics [@kraft2015a; @kraft2015; @lohbeck2014a; @norden2015; @chang2019].\

\noindent Here, we provide the first comprehensive view of trait succession across a wide temporal and biogeographic scale, aiming to answer three fundamental questions [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]: (1) What are the dominant environmental feedback dynamics for determining successional trait trajectories? (2) What are the mechanisms of abiotic feedback dynamics with respect to filtering and modulating traits? (3) How does the individual sensitivity of traits to abiotic feedback mechanisms induce successional variability across environments?\

\noindent To archive this, we focused on eight traits representing functional cluster and key aspects of successional strategies [@maynard2022]: Bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, seed dry mass, shade tolerance, specific leaf area, tree height, and wood density. We used machine learning to model the expression of these traits as a function of standage and environmental predictors, utilizing data from more than 55 000 forest plots across the United States. We then predicted the trajectory of traits through succession and explored scenarios of both high and low abiotic conditions.\

\noindent We hypothesize (1) abiotic feedback mechanisms to have a higher relative importance [@kraft2015a]; (2) filtering to be the dominant mechanism of abiotic feedback dynamics [@bouchard2024; @boukili2017], and (3) variability across environments to be induced by strong abiotic modulation (and not filtering) of traits linked to competition for resources, such as those governing light acquisition [@poorter2008; @kunstler2016]. By answering these questions, we aim to better understand how the environment drives community assembly through the lens of traits, contributing towards a unified theory of succession. \newpage

# MATERIAL AND METHODS

## **Data collection and preparation**

### Forest Data

We retrieved tree occurrence data from the US Forest Inventory Analysis [@grayForestInventoryAnalysis2012], a long-term dataset dating back to the 1930s and spanning 13 biomes. We first filtered 37 984 uniformly sampled plots across the United States (excluding Hawaii) with known standage, inventory year, and latitude longitude coordinates. In the FIA, standage is approximated based on the diameter and height of the most abundant age class [@grayForestInventoryAnalysis2012], and a commonly used proxy for successional stages. We excluded plots that fall in the upper 10% quantile of standage to avoid the impact of extremely old plots, focusing our analysis on general age ranges for early to late stage succession in the United States ranging from 1--150 years [@wilfahrt2014; @oliverForestStandDynamics1996]. Privately owned plots and those actively managed by forestry were also excluded. Up to three repeated measures of the same plots were included if their inventory years were at least 10 years apart. Georeferenced plots were then grouped into biomes using RESOLVE biome codes (see supplementary material \ref{tab:S3}), and plots from biomes with less than 100 observations were excluded, yielding 55 985 plots for the analysis (Fig. \ref{fig:fig1}).

### Tree Traits

To quantify the expression of functional tree traits for plots at a given standage, we integrated available trait data with trees identified to the species level. Tree height was retrieved from the FIA data where available, and we integrated species-specific estimates of bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, seed dry mass, specific leaf area, and wood density from a cleaned and phylogenetically imputed subset of the TRY database (v. 5.0) [@kattge2020a] available from @maynard2022. Additionally, we included an index of species-specific shade tolerance from @rueda2018 after imputing missing values following a phylogenetic nearest-neighbour lookup using *phylomaker* in R [@jin2019], yielding a set of traits that capture functionalities of the stem (bark thickness, wood density), leaf economics (specific leaf area, leaf nitrogen), water transportation (conduit diameter), growth and light acquisition (tree height, shade tolerance), and reproduction (seed dry mass) (see supplementary material \ref{tab:S1} for trait descriptives).

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=3.76in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig1.png}
\caption{Heatmap showing the number of plots included in the analysis across the United States (main map showing the US mainland, bottom left map showing Alaska). Note that polygons may extend into the ocean due to the density smoothing, but no plots where actually measured in the ocean.}
\label{fig:fig1}
\end{figure}
```
\noindent Trait values were log-transformed and scaled around zero to stabilize variance and allow for unitless comparison across traits. We summarized the trait expression per species per plot as the weighted plot mean of trait values across trees belonging to one species, weighted by the species-specific basal area scaled to the number of trees of one species per acre. Scaled basal area was calculated as:

$$
\text{basal area (scaled)} = \left[\pi \times \left(\frac{\text{diameter}}{2}\right)^2\right] \times \frac{n_{\text{tree}}}{\text{acre}},
$$

where $n_{\text{tree}}$ is the number of trees of a given species found in a plot.\

\noindent Finally, we calculated the trait expression per plot as the weighted mean of species-per-plot trait values, weighted by the sum of scaled basal areas per species.

### Environmental Features

Environmental predictors were selected to capture key abiotic conditions affecting tree distribution and productivity [@maynard2022]. We retrieved climate, elevation, and soil characteristic from a global composite [@vandenhoogen2019]. Climate variables originated from the CHELSEA data set [@karger2017] and included annual mean temperature, minimum, maximum, and mean temperatures of the coldest and warmest months, annual precipitation, and precipitation of the wettest, driest, warmest, and coldest quarters. Soil characteristics were retrieved from the SoilGrid250 layer [@hengl2017] and included sand content, water capacity, and pH at 15cm and 60cm depths. Elevation data was obtained from @amatulli2018. The georeferenced data were resampled in a unified pixel grid at a 30 arcsec resolution (EPSG:4326 (WGS84) at ca. 1 km$^2$) and values were extracted for every latitude and longitude coordinate of our forest plots [@vandenhoogen2019] by averaging the mean values.\

\noindent To reduce high collinearity among environmental variables and to avoid unrealistic scenarios in trait predictions, we performed a principal component analysis (PCA) to identify underlying axes across temperature, precipitation, and soil variables. We excluded soil pH from the PCA due to its variability in both sandy and water-retentive soils, and elevation to directly control for its effect and simplify the interpretation of the principal components. We fitted the PCA model after testing suitability using the KMO criterion and Bartlett's test of sphericity, identified three principal components with eigenvalues greater than one (see supplementary supplementary material \ref{fig:S4}a), and rotated their loadings using varimax rotation for clearer interpretation.\

\noindent The first component, accounting for 42.1% of the variance, was strongly positively related to temperature variables. The second component, accounting for 23.9% of the variance, was negatively related to sand content and positively related to water capacity. The third component, accounting for 17.8% of the variance, was positively related to precipitation variables (see \ref{fig:S4} b.--d. for details; and \ref{tab:S2} for feature descriptives).

## Data Analysis

We used random forest regression in the *ranger* package [@wright2017] in R to model trait expression as a function of standage and environmental predictors within a machine learning framework. We also included binary variables representing biome association of plots to account for biogeographic differences. This approach allowed us to accurately assess predictability and capture potential non-linearity in trait trajectories. Random forest regression is an ensemble learning method that builds multiple decision trees and averages their predictions to estimate trait values more accurately and robustly. The algorithm relies on a specified set of hyperparameters that, among others, determine splitting rules, variable selection, and stopping criteria for each tree. To ensure model reliability, we split the data into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets, and performed hyperparameter tuning on each trait model using cross-validation across a grid of hyperparameters (see supplementary material \ref{fig:S5}) in the *caret* package [@kuhn2008] to optimize model performance. We then selected the set of hyperparameters yielding the lowest prediction error and fitted trait models using the calibrated hyperparameters and the full dataset.\

\noindent To understand the directionality and relative importance of features, we computed shapley values from the trait models using 100 simulations with the fastshap package [@greenfastshap2024]. Shapley values use a game theory based approach to measure each variable's impact on the outcome for every data point by considering the influence of all features in the model, similarly to partial regression [@marcilio2020; @shapley1997]. For every model, we then ranked the features by their overall importance, calculated as the sum of their absolute shapley values (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) and calculated the mean rank per feature. \newpage

\noindent We then investigated relationships between traits and environmental features using partial dependence, which calculates the marginal effect of a variable on the predicted outcome, allowing us to understand how changes in that feature influence the model's predictions while averaging out the effects of other features [@friedmanGreedyFunctionApproximation2001]. We fitted partial dependence curves using the *pdp* package [@greenwellPdpPackageConstructing2017] for our global trait models and calculated the successional signal of traits as the difference between the trait expression at the last predicted standage (150 years) and the curves' intercept (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b, note that the visualized curves are smoothed by fitting a Generalized Additive Model with cubic spline smoothing). We then compared the signal between traits, assuming a high importance of abiotic feedbacks if the signal was low, and a high importance of biotic feedbacks if the signal was high.\

\noindent Next, we stratified our data based on the upper and lower 25% quantiles of environmental features to create "high" and "low" scenario subsets, effectively capturing more extreme variations in features while minimizing the influence of outliers. We then used the stratified data to fit novel random forest models for all traits in every scenario and calculated partial dependence curves for each trait in every scenario (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}). This approach offered several advantages over the more common approach of predicting traits across an environmental grid using the global models. First, it restricted predictions to observed combinations of features, avoiding extrapolation into unobserved environmental conditions. Second, it implicitly captured non-linear patterns and interaction effects between environmental features and biomes, which were evident based on significant two-way ANOVA tests (all *p* \< 0.05). Third, it allowed us to assess the sensitivity of model predictions and to quantify predictive uncertainty across different environmental conditions. Finally, this method enabled us to capture potential heterogeneous effects of environmental features on trait outcomes, allowing for the possibility that a feature could have a strong positive effect on the trait outcome in the lower quantile, but a weaker or negative effect in the upper quantile. \newpage

\noindent Using the stratified partial dependence data, we retrieved the predicted intercept and calculated the successional signal of traits for each feature scenario after visually confirming that the trajectories were sufficiently linear to make this value meaningful (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}). We then calculated the differences in intercepts and successional signals between feature scenarios, both averaged across traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a.) and for individual traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b). This allowed us to assess the magnitude of differences at the intercept (indicative of abiotic filtering at the outset of succession) and the signal (indicative of abiotic modulation throughout succession) between high and low feature scenarios. We then positioned every trait in a space of abiotic filtering and modulation, represented by the mean intercept difference and the mean signal difference across feature scenarios (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c). This allowed us to identify the dominant mechanisms driving successional differences across environments in individual traits.\

\noindent To evaluate the abiotic predictability (and thereby determinism) versus variability of different trait trajectories across environments, we retrieved the $\textit{R}^2$ values of every model. It is important to note that these $\textit{R}^2$ values include standage as a predictive variable, as standage was used to predict trait expressions. Therefore, they do not reflect the pure predictive power of abiotic features. We also fitted random forest models for every traits across all feature scenarios excluding standage and recorded their respective $\textit{R}^2$ values to quantify the extent to which the inclusion of standage influenced our approximation of abiotic predictability (see supplementary material \ref{fig:S9}).\

\noindent Finally, we compared the magnitude of abiotic filtering and modulating for individual traits with the mean $\textit{R}^2$ of the respective models, allowing us to position traits in a space of high and low importance of abiotic filtering (x axis in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a) or modulating (x axis in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b) and its predictability (y axis in \ref{fig:fig4}). This allowed us to to understand which mechanisms contributed to environmental variability in which traits and assess their predictability. All analyses were carried out in R v.4.3.2. [@rcore2023]. \newpage

# RESULTS

## **Importance of environmental features**

The 55 841 forest plots used in this study had standages ranging from 1--150 years at a mean of 75.71 $\pm$ 33.2 years ($\pm$ standard deviation) and spanned 8 different biomes. In our global models, environmental features along with biome association and standage explained a substantial proportion of variance across most traits (mean $\textit{R}^2$ = 0.65 $\pm$ 0.04), with only the model for tree height having an $\textit{R}^2$ below 0.6 (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.).

### Abiotic tree-environment feedback dynamics

\noindent Across all trait models, temperature (mean rank 1.63 $\pm$ 0.38 ($\pm$ standard error), Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.) and elevation (mean rank 2.63 $\pm$ 0.38, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.) emerged as the most influential features, consistently ranking in the top three predictors for all traits except shade tolerance (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Temperature was the primary factor for five traits (wood density, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, seed dry mass), and followed a consistent directionality: higher temperatures led to increased expressions of traits, while lower temperatures had the opposite effect (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Cold temperatures particularly reduced the expression of leaf-related traits and conduit diameter (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.), which together with bark thickness and seed dry mass showed particularly strong increases at higher temperatures (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Elevation exhibited similar effects, with stem-related traits showing a pronounced negative response to lower elevations and conduit diameter displaying a strong increase at higher elevations (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Precipitation (mean rank 3.75 $\pm$ 0.53, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.) and pH (mean rank 4.25 $\pm$ 0.41, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.) also consistently ranked high, placing in the top three features for three traits and in the top five for seven (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Precipitation was particularly important for determining shade tolerance, and generally increased traits at higher values while decreasing them at lower values (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). We observed a similar trend for pH, which had a notably strong effect in decreasing tree height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). The soil's capacity to retain water versus its sandiness showed a lower importance (mean rank 6.375 $\pm$ 0.18, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.) and lacked a strong directionality for most traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a).

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=6.9in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig2.png}
\caption[Shortened Caption]{a. Shapley values of best fitting trait models (left sub-plots) indicating relative feature importance and directionality. Features are arranged by their overall importance in descending order (right sub-plots). Features include biome association (B-BF = Boreal Forests or Taiga, B-FG = Flooded Grasslands, B-MW = Mediterranean Woodlands, B-TB = Temperate Broadleaf Forest, B-TC = Temperate Coniferous Forest, B-TG = Temperate Grasslands, B-TU = Tundra, B-XS = Xeric Shrublands), elevation (ELEV), pH of the soil (pH), precipitation qualities (PRCP (PC)), soil qualities (SOIL (PC), i.e., water-retention vs sandiness of the soil), standage (STAGE), and}
\label{fig:fig2}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]\ContinuedFloat
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption[]{temperature qualities (TEMP (PC)). Negative shapley values indicate a negative effect of a given feature on the respective trait expression, positive shapley values a positive effect. High and low feature values are indicated by the colour gradient. b. Partial dependence curves of traits across standage. Signal per trait (in the plot titles) indicates the difference between late-stage succession (150 years) and early succession (0 years) on a log-scale. Stronger signal hints towards lower importance of abiotic feedback mechanisms.}
\end{figure}
```
\noindent Biome association generally did not play a major role in explaining trait expression (mean ranks for Coniferous Forest = 5.5 $\pm$ 0.9, Temperate Broadleaf Forest = 6.8 $\pm$ 0.5, Temperate Grassland = 9 $\pm$ 0, Boreal Forest or Taiga = 10.5 $\pm$ 0.27, Xeric Shrublands = 10.8 $\pm$ 0.16, Mediterranean Woodlands = 12.63 $\pm$ 0.46, Tundra = 12.3 $\pm$ 0.32, Flooded Grasslands = 13.5 $\pm$ 0.19, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) with the exception of bark thickness, where whether a plot was located in a temperate coniferous forest or not was as important a predictor as temperature (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.).

### Biotic tree-environment feedbacks

\noindent Standage, representing changing biotic feedback dynamics causing turnover across succession, had a relatively low importance for most traits (mean rank 5.5 $\pm$ 1.0) and only showed relevance for shade tolerance and tree height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Standage was the single most important feature determining tree height, with taller trees associated with increased standage (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Similarly, standage increased shade tolerance and contributed substantially towards explaining its expression (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.).

### Relative importance of feedbacks dynamics

Overall, abiotic features played a dominant role in explaining most traits and showed a substantially greater importance in determining trait outcomes than standage, with the exception of tree height and shade tolerance (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.). Comparisons of the successional signal (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.) reinforced this trend, as we only observed a strong signal in tree height (Signal = 0.84 on a log-scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.) and shade tolerance (Signal = 0.39 on a log-scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.). Seed dry mass (Signal = 0.16 on a log-scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b) and wood density (Signal = 0.12 on a log-scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.) showed a lower, but noteworthy signal. \newpage

\noindent All other traits had an absolute signal of 0.05 or lower (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.). With most traits increasing across successional time (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.), bark thickness and leaf nitrogen were the only traits with negative predicted trajectories, i.e., marginal decreases with standage (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b.).

## **Feedback Mechanisms**

Most trait trajectories exhibited approximately linear trends or saturating relationships with stand age across feature scenarios with few directional shifts (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}).\

\noindent Comparisons of mean intercepts and successional signals between feature (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a.) reinforced the strong influence of feedbacks to temperature (mean intercept difference = 0.19 $\pm$ 0.05 ($\pm$ standard error, both on a log-scale), mean signal difference = 0.27 $\pm$ 0.04, \ref{fig:fig3}a) and elevation (mean intercept difference = 0.22 $\pm$ 0.07, mean signal difference = 0.27 $\pm$ 0.06, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a.) observed in the global models. These features heavily filtered traits at the outset of succession, and even more strongly modulated successional trajectories in especially high and low temperature conditions (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b. and c.). Temperature was a particularly strong filter for stem-related traits (intercept difference for bark thickness = 0.38 and wood-density = 0.43, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.), and exerted moderate filtering effects on other traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.). Elevation exerted the strongest filtering on tree height (intercept difference 0.33, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.), shade tolerance (intercept difference 0.41, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.), and seed dry mass (intercept difference 0.53, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.). Temperature and elevation heavily modulated the trajectories of all traits except bark thickness (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c).\

\noindent Precipitation was the strongest abiotic filter across traits (mean intercept difference = 0.39 $\pm$ 0.09, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.), substantially filtering all traits except height and seed dry mass, and particularly filtering shade tolerance (intercept difference 0.85, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.). However, modulation effects were less pronounced, as the successional signal of all traits showed little variation between high and low precipitation scenarios (0.12 $\pm$ 0.01, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c.).\

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=8.43in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig3.png}
\caption[Shortened Caption]{Filtering vs. modulation effects of features on trait succession. a. Mean difference}
\label{fig:fig3}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]\ContinuedFloat
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption[]{between intercept and signal of trait trajectories across high and low feature scenarios. b. Differences between high and low feature scenarios per trait in the intercept and c. in the signal. d. Trait positioning in a space of filtering versus modualtion effects.}
\end{figure}
```
\noindent Soil pH was a stronger filter than the soil's capacity to retain water (intercept difference soil pH = 0.26 $\pm$ 0.04, soil water retention = 0.16 $\pm$ 0.03, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b.) and only (but strongly) modulated tree height through succession (signal difference = 0.44, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c.). Water retention of the soil showed no strong modulation effects (mean signal difference = 0.08 $\pm$ 0.01), and neither did pH (mean signal difference = 0.11 $\pm$ 0.05) for other traits than height.\

\noindent Overall, we observed most traits clustering around intermediate filtering and modulation effects, suggesting no clear trade-off when averaged across conditions in bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, seed dry mass, specific leaf area, and wood density (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}d). Filtering and modulating only seemed to be mutually excluding at the dimensions of tree height and shade tolerance, where the heavily modulated tree height showed little sensitivity to filtering, and the strongly filtered shade tolerance little sensitivity to modulation (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}d).

## **Predictability versus variability across environments**

We did not observe a clear trend in abiotic filtering or modulating with respect to predictability. Well-predictable traits mostly aligned with greater filtering than modulation effects, even tough modulation was still present. This was the case for bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, and wood density (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a.). In contemporary communities, successional variability is likely to be primarily induced through abiotic filters at these trait dimensions (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b.), most noteworthy temperature and elevation (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a.) and most strongly evident for bark thickness. This pattern held also true for shade tolerance, yet at a lower predictability (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}). The opposite was true only for tree height, which was generally less predictable. When comparing abiotic feedback mechanisms, differences in tree height are more likely induced by modulation at a low determinism (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b.), while initial conditions have little influence on height trajectories (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a.). Overall, successional variability may have different abiotic causes at different trait dimensions. Highly deterministic traits may show regional differences due to strong abiotic filters (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a, see also supplementary material \ref{fig:S7}) but they may also show divergence in successional trajectories between particularly high and low temperature and elevation environments. These differences should be predictable and therefore deterministic for traits other than height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b, see also supplementary material \ref{fig:S8}), where biotic dynamics are more important. The $\textit{R}^2$ of models excluding stand age left these relationships largely unchanged. Explanatory power decreased by a mean of 0.02 $\pm$ 0.003, with the $\textit{R}^2$ of only four models decreasing between 0.1 and 0.17 (supplementary material \ref{fig:S9}). \

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=3.99in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig4.png}
\caption{Trait positioning in a space of a.: abiotic filtering (abiotic differences in the intercept, i.e., successional outset), and b.: abiotic modulation (abiotic differences in the signal, i.e, successional trajectories) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$). Points resemble the mean values per trait and the error bars the standard errors for the respective axes.}
\label{fig:fig4}
\end{figure}
```
# DISCUSSION

This work provides the first comprehensive view of functional trait succession across extensive temporal and biogeographic scales. While succession is one of the most thoroughly studied spatio-temporal processes driving forest diversity patterns [@christensen2014], the roles and mechanisms of feedback dynamics between trees and their environment remain poorly understood [@chang2019; @vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. In this study, we partitioned succession into distinct trait trajectories and found that abiotic conditions, particularly temperature and elevation, play a dominant and predictable role in filtering and modulating the successional trajectories of most traits.\

\noindent Consistent with our first hypothesis, our results show that traits related to stem structure, leaf economics, water transpiration, and reproductive strategies are predominantly assembled by feedbacks to abiotic conditions limiting functional turnover across succession. In contrast, only traits associated with resource competition, such as tree height and shade tolerance, are primarily assembled by changing biotic dynamics, promoting functional turnover. Partly supporting our second hypothesis, we found that temperature, elevation, and precipitation are strong filters selecting for functional groups capable of establishing at the outset of succession, and that traits overall appeared more susceptible to filtering than modulation. However, we observed strong modulation in environments with varying temperature and elevation in nearly all traits.Contrary to our third hypothesis, abiotic filters significantly contribute to environmental variability in traits, especially those related to stem, leaf, transpiration, and reproduction. Additional variability is introduced by diverging trait trajectories across temperature and elevation conditions, yet both filtering and modulation appear predictable and deterministic. Traits related to resource competition, however, may introduce less predictable, more site-specific variability in succession, particularly under different temperature and elevation conditions. \

\noindent The directionality of trait trajectories observed in this study largely align with previous research on trait succession. In addition to the expected increases in shade tolerance and tree height, we also noted a less pronounced but consistent increase in wood density and seed dry mass. These trends are consistent with earlier findings linking them to shifting trade-offs between resource acquisition and conservation as succession progresses [@boukili2017; @douma2012; @poorter2008; @wilfahrt2014]. This shift may also explain the slight but consistent decrease in leaf nitrogen we observed, a trend previously associated with reduced photosynthetic performance in old-growth forests [@boukili2017; @lohbeck2014a]. While specific leaf area has been reported to respond to stand age in certain biomes [@douma2012; @wilfahrt2014], its effect and directionality were not evident in this study when averaged across environments. The same was true for bark thickness and conduit diameter, traits for which there is limited literature on successional trajectories.\

\noindent Our findings reinforce the established consensus that abiotic conditions play a crucial role in shaping plant diversity patterns [@cornwell2009; @kraft2015a; @suding2008]. Temperature and elevation have long been recognized as critical factors influencing plant diversity patterns [@whittaker1960; @woodward1987; @korner2007], and we demonstrate this to be true at a functional level across extensive temporal and biogeographic scales. Recent research often highlighted temperature as the most significant factor in explaining diversity patterns, both for plants in general [@moles2014] and for trees specifically, also at the functional level [@maynard2022; @paz2024]. We expand on this, showing that temperature and elevation not only act as filters for functional groups but also mediate successional trajectories across different environments. This finding is particularly concerning, as temperature, unlike elevation, is a fluctuating condition that is undergoing significant changes due to ongoing climate change [@arnell2019]. Therefore, temperature may not only filter trees based on their thermal safety margins [@osullivan2017], but also increase community divergence by influencing functional turnover across trait dimensions. To date, climate warming has been empirically shown to increase divergence of successional pathways in microbial communities [@guoClimateWarmingLeads2018], but not yet in the more sessile domain of forests. Similarly to @guoClimateWarmingLeads2018, we suggest that temperature-induced divergence is predictable in most trait dimensions, except for height and shade tolerance, where stochasticity may be higher. We show that precipitation, which is also affected by climate change [@ranaUnderstandingJointBehavior2017], strongly filters functional traits in early succession, but unlike temperature, shows little effect on modulating trajectories. In contrast, the soil's capacity to retain water had a negligible effect on most traits and exhibited minimal filtering or modulation capabilities. The role of precipitation in influencing functional diversity has been reported with varying magnitudes [@maynard2022; @moles2014], and also its utility as a proxy for water availability has been questioned [@hickler2009; @moles2014]. In this study, we found that precipitation surpasses the soil's capacity to retain water in filtering functional groups, but neither factor appears to modulate successional pathways. Consequently, forests may still exhibit divergence if precipitation patterns become more disparate with climate change, but this is likely to occur independently of successional time.\

\noindent Overall, these findings have significant implications for future forest management aimed at meeting conservation goals under different climate scenarios [@borner2020]. As climate conditions shift, the functional profiles of many forests - particularly in terms of stem, leaf, transpiration, and seed traits - will be subject to a novel and fundamental abiotic selection. This could exacerbate the already observed and increasing global die-offs of forests due to heat stress [@anderegg2013]. Moreover, even forest communities that pass these initial filters may continue to face challenges if temperature shifts drive functional divergence over succession. However, we predict that temperature-induced divergence will be deterministic and predictable, allowing forest management to adapt to varying climate scenarios. This underscores the need for further research to establish clear relationships between temperature changes and community divergence to better prepare for successional shifts induced by climate change.\

\noindent The study of trait succession made rapid advances in the past years, but several limitations should be acknowledged and aimed to overcome in the future. When looking at traits across large spatial scales, intraspecific trait variation is often neglected [@albertIntraspecificFunctionalVariability2010]. Here, we used species estimates of traits which biases results towards a more conservative estimate of trait dynamics, but obscures the potential contribution of intraspecific variation to trait trajectories. Similar may be said about ontogenetic variation, i.e., changes in trait expressions as trees progress through different stages of their lifespan [@poorterLeafSizeLeaf2008; @thomasPhotosyntheticDifferencesSaplings2002]. These may also contribute temporal dynamics in trait trajectories or influence successional pathways, and would require longitudinal studies including lifestage-specific trait data to incorporate a more comprehensive view of trait evolution over time [@kunstlerGrowthMortalityMorphological2005].\

\noindent Although machine learning approaches are increasingly popular in ecology [@christinApplicationsDeepLearning2019] and enable analyses beyond traditional methods, techniques like random forests remain correlative and lack the ability to establish causality or parametric relationships [@cutlerRandomForestsClassification2007]. They also don't allow for hierarchical structures, and thus for example the inclusion of space as a random effect. Future modelling approaches within a machine learning framework should therefore also consider spatial cross-validation to improve generalizability and enhance the robustness of predictions. This becomes especially important if the forest data is autocorrelated in space and not as spatially balanced as the FIA data used in this study. Also, the inclusion of more traits may enhance our understanding of trait succession.\

\noindent We highlighted the critical role that tree feedbacks to abiotic conditions play in shaping succession from the outset through its progression. The variability in trait responses to different conditions underscores the complexity of succession, and forecasts the vulnerability of contemporary forests to climate change, especially regenerating forests undergoing successional dynamics. As temperature and precipitation regimes continue to shift, refining these models will be crucial for predicting how existing forests will respond and for developing adaptive management strategies to mitigate threads and to promote the resilience of global forests.\

------------------------------------------------------------------------

\
\noindent \textbf{ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS}. My special gratitude is to my supervisor Dr. Daniel S. Maynard who guided and supported me through the entire process of this project. I also thank Alastair Pickering for providing the processed shade tolerance data. Lastly, I thank the University College London for supporting and facilitating this project.\

\noindent \textbf{CODE AND DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT}. The FIA, trait, and environmental data used in this study are openly available in the online supplementary materials of the corresponding publications. All code and the cleaned data used in this study are openly available in the designated [GitHub repository](https://github.com/MerlinWe/succession_traits).\

\noindent \textbf{NOTE FROM THE AUTHOR}. Although this manuscript uses the first person plural to align with common language in scientific publishing, it was solely written by the author mentioned. ChatGPT was used for debugging and improving code efficiency. No AI tool was used to write any code (or text) from scratch.

```{=tex}
\AtEndDocument{
\pagebreak

\section*{SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 1-3: Variable descriptives}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{S\arabic{table}}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Descriptive properties of trait values as they were used in this analyis. Histograms show their respective distribution. Remaining columns show their respecitve mean, minimum, and maximum values, and the unit as used in this analysis (all on a log-scale).}
\includegraphics[width=6.5in, height=8.07in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/trait_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S1}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Descriptive properties of abiotic features as used in this analysis. Histograms show their respective distribution. Remaining columns show their respecitve mean, minimum, and maximum values, and the unit as used in the analysis}
\includegraphics[width=6in, height=4.1in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/feature_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S2}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Biome associations of plots recorded in this study. Table shows count of occurrences per biome.}
\includegraphics[width=5.5in, height=3.1in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/biome_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S3}
\end{table}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 4: Principal Component Analysis}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{figure}{3} 

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.24in, height=4.08in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s4.png}
\caption{Principal component analysis of abiotic variabales. Variables included in the PCA were the sand content of the soil at 15 and 60 cm depth, the water capacity of the soil at 15 and 60 cm depth, annual mean temperature, minimum, maximum, and mean temperatures of the coldest and warmest months, annual precipitation, and precipitation of the wettest, driest, warmest, and coldest quarters. a. Percentage of variance explained by calculated principal components. 3 components showed an eigenvalue greater than 1, i.e., explained a greater amount of variance than a single variable. Together these components explained 83.8 percent of the total variance across abiotic variables included b.- d. Varibale contributions to unique components with an eigenvalue greater than 1 after loadings were rotated using varimax rotation. The first component (b.) showed strong positive association with all temperature varibles. The second component (c.) showed strong positive association with the soil's capacity to retain water, and strong negative association with a higher sand content. The third component (d.) showed strong positive association with all precipitation variables included.}
\label{fig:S4}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 5: Hyperparameter Tuning}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.24in, height=4.08in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s5.png}
\caption{Heatmaps showing the performance of the hyperparameters ‘number of trees’ (the facets), ‘minimum node size’, and ‘number of variables included at each splitting point (mtry)’ in model tuning. Displayed is the prediction error for random forest models across the eight traits included: wood density, bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, seed dry mass, shade tolerance, and tree height. Each subplot corresponds to a specific number of trees (500, 1000, 1500), with the x-axis representing mtry and the y-axis representing the minimum node size. The color of each tile reflects the prediction error, with darker colors indicating lower errors and thus better model performance. The patterns across the heatmaps reveal optimal hyperparameter combinations for each trait, helping to identify the tuning that minimize prediction error and enhance model accuracy.}
\label{fig:S5}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 6: Partial Dependence Curves of Feature Scenarios}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=5.33in, height=7.36in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s6.png}
\captionof{figure}{Partial dependence curves of traits in all high and low environmental scenarios (a.–e.) and their predictive power (f.). Curves are smoothed by fitting a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) with cubic spline smoothing.}
\label{fig:S6}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 7: Intercept Differences per Trait and per Feature}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.02in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s7.png}
\captionof{figure}{Trait positioning in a space of abiotic filtering (abiotic differences in the intercept, i.e., successional outset) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$ of both scenarios) for every feature. Points resemble the trait values.}
\label{fig:S7}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 8: Signal Differences per Trait and per Feature}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.02in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s8.png}
\captionof{figure}{Trait positioning in a space of abiotic modulation (abiotic differences in the signal, i.e., successional trajectory) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$ of both scenarios) for every feature. Points resemble the trait values.}
\label{fig:S8}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 9: Difference in scenario model accuracy with standage exclusion}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.13in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s9.png}
\captionof{figure}{Changes in the $\textit{R}^2$ of scenario models if standage was not included as a predictive feature. If standage is excluded, the $\textit{R}^2$ resembles the true environmental predictability by only abiotic features.}
\label{fig:S9}
\end{figure}

}
```
